oxc-project/oxc#14989.body:
Original issue Hi. I am using Windows 11 and oxfmt 0.8.0 with the following `.oxfmtrc.json`: ```json { "semi": true, "singleQuote": true, "useTabs": true } ``` It's adding new line to every single imports and more (in JS objects). I checked the config options https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/597c9e8804d0ff85b22b67617c2c7e3833e2a4d3/napi/playground/src/options.rs and see nothing to configure those new lines, and it feels like a bug to me. The original final: ```ts import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; import { QueryClientProvider } from '@tanstack/react-query'; import { LinksProvider, VuiProvider } from '@veracity/vui'; import React from 'react'; import { HelmetProvider } from 'react-helmet-async'; import { MemoryRouter } from 'react-router'; import 'src/styles/global.css'; import { queryClient } from '../src/libs/queryClient'; const preview: Preview = { decorators: [ (Story) => ( ), ], parameters: { controls: { matchers: { color: /(background|color)$/i, date: /Date$/i, }, }, a11y: { test: 'todo', }, }, }; export default preview; ```
```js const x = 1; const y = 2; // ↑ CRLF ↓ LF const z = 3; ``` Given this code as `t.ts`. (Be sure to add CRLF for first line break.) Then, `cargo run -p oxc_formatter --example formatter t.ts` to reproduce. ```js const x = 1; const y = 2; // ↑ CRLF ↓ LF const z = 3; ``` I suspect the root cause is `line_before` and `line_after` for `JoinNodesBuilder` does not consider CRLF.
oxc-project/oxc#14989.title: formatter: unexpected new lines behavior with CRLF

oxc-project/oxc#15007.body:
### What version of Oxlint are you using?

latest

### What command did you run?

Through VSCode's oxc 1.24.0 extension

### What does your `.oxlintrc.json` config file look like?

_No response_

### What happened?

One my TypeScript file has the following import:
```typescript
import * as z from 'zod';
```
Another one has:
```typescript
import type * as DrizzleKit from 'drizzle-kit/api';
```

Both lines are getting flagged by the `import/import-no-namespace` rule. This is a rule I'd like to use, but as far as Zod is concerned, importing the whole namespace is the recommended way.

Now instead of littering my code with `// oxlint-disable-line import/no-namespace` each time I use Zod (which seems to trigger Copilot too), I figured I would add an exception for that rule straight into my `.oxlintrc.json`.

[The documentation](https://oxc.rs/docs/guide/usage/linter/rules/import/import-no-namespace.html#options) confuses me though (besides `ignore` being spelled `ignores` in the example):

`ignore`: array of glob strings for modules that should be ignored by the rule :
```json
{
  "ignore": ["*.json"]
}
```

I can't read Rust for the life of me, so I'm not sure what's going on with the `ignore` parameter, but this:
```
"import/no-namespace": ["warn", { "ignore": ["zod"] }],
```
will disable the rule for BOTH `'zod'` and `'drizzle-kit/api'`, whereas it should only disable it for `'zod'` and still warn me about the second one.
 
What kind of globbing is really performed here?

Thank you
oxc-project/oxc#15007.title: linter: is `import/import-no-namespace`'s `ignore` option working properly?

oxc-project/oxc#15073.body:
### What version of Oxlint are you using?

1.24.0

### What command did you run?

Both VSCode extension and command line

### What does your `.oxlintrc.json` config file look like?

*Config is not related to this issue*

### What happened?

For example, we have a `.vue` file (It also happens to `.astro` and `.svelte` file)
```vue
<script>
// This is a line of very very normal code comment, nothing special here.
const somemessage
</script>
```

And run oxlint
```bash
$ oxlint ignored.vue

  × Missing initializer in const declaration
   ╭─[ignored.vue:2:73]
 1 │     <script>
 2 │ ╭─▶ // This is a line of very very normal code comment, nothing special here.
 3 │ ╰─▶ const somemessage
 4 │     </script>
   ╰────
```

And you will find, the thrown error by oxlint is in the second line, but the syntax errors is actually in the third line

Only the errors from parser have this issue, it seems that the errors aren't added offset, also the `<script>` tag's width by default

And It can even make oxc_language_server panic 

```vue
<script>
// 你好你好你好你好你好你好你好你好
const message
</script>
```

Since the error location is on the second line, so oxlint would try to find the byte in the second line, and if the target char is not an English letter or a number, it may cause panic like this

```bash
thread 'main' panicked at crates/oxc_data_structures/src/rope.rs:13:35:
byte index 59 is not a char boundary; it is inside '好' (bytes 57..60) of `<script>
// 你好你好你好你好你好你好你好你好
const message
</script>
```

I will make up a PR to solve it soon
oxc-project/oxc#15073.title: linter: partial loading file throw errors with wrong position and may cause panic

oxc-project/oxc#15078.body:
There is no way currently to generate the config docs correctly for a rule like [prefer_es6_class](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/55ee9627932e4b28f62a43dcf6f99a99cf945a00/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/react/prefer_es6_class.rs). In this case, there isn't a config object, but rather just a string value for the mode to run the rule in:

```json
"react/prefer-es6-class": ["error", "always"]
```

Right now the auto-generated config doesn't really handle this pattern correctly and we need to update the website generation logic to allow this.

Other rules with this behavior that need this change:

- [`vue/define-emits-declaration`](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/aafe08c47d96adb9dad326bc7489b6c830f03652/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/vue/define_emits_declaration.rs)
- [`vue/define-props-declaration`](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/2ed5059847f791867832193a2b94943532e1e544/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/vue/define_props_declaration.rs) (although this is technically defined as taking a bool, which is somewhat wrong?)
- [`eslint/operator-assignment`](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/ace4def45e82d715aa631578c6d9835830987d65/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/eslint/operator_assignment.rs)
- [`jest/no_restricted_matchers.rs`](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/7660a883238a86d1639af22ac269fafe66d3caad/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/jest/no_restricted_matchers.rs) this one isn't string-based, but does have the same problem.
- [`eslint/yoda`](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/c45a080e94e448b019ca3404eb8dd5076f9219d7/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/eslint/yoda.rs) accepts a string argument of `always`
- [`eslint/func-names`](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/c45a080e94e448b019ca3404eb8dd5076f9219d7/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/eslint/func_names.rs) - this supports one string config option and then a keyword argument as well.
- [`unicorn/switch-case-braces`](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/main/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/unicorn/switch_case_braces.rs)
- [`import/extensions`](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/410b3cb4e9d5a4add6ac75b54c0bbf02d109be40/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/import/extensions.rs), accepts both a string or an object.
- [`eslint/no-restricted-globals`](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/2c589522117f7631e4c588d62109f90fa99354d3/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/eslint/no_restricted_globals.rs), this currently has incorrect docs because of this problem.
- [`typescript/consistent-indexed-object-style`](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/a66ef665e72e7902a024e1b4786a80ba6ce1e784/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/typescript/consistent_indexed_object_style.rs)
- [`typescript/consistent-type-definitions`](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/667edb1d08527a318fc0c6c4e52abb7c77a8b527/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/typescript/consistent_type_definitions.rs)
oxc-project/oxc#15078.title: linter: Improve config generation for website to support string-based configs

oxc-project/oxc#15095.body:
See #14743 for the overall effort around this. We want to make sure that auto-generated documentation is ensured by CI, so new rules cannot regress on this behavior.

Similar to [rule_configuration_test.rs](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/63687935f73aae6bf46c103b2d6846f138944d57/crates/oxc_linter/tests/rule_configuration_test.rs), a test script should be added to ensure that all rules with configuration options have a Configuration block in their rule page on the website.

We can start by enforcing this per-plugin, with all `oxc/` rules that have config options now being fully documented, and hopefully soon import, jest, promise, jsdoc, and vue also being able to be added to the enforced plugins in the next few weeks. `react-perf` and `nextjs` are also fully covered at this point, I believe.

I'm not 100% sure how this should be done, ideally it'd be able to enforce that every config option in the file has corresponding documentation, but at the least we should enforce for now that all rules which define a `from_configuration` function also have a `Configuration` section in their generated website page. [doc_page.rs](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/c51c036e5a5cecee92f9357d2b831fa8497a8f53/tasks/website/src/linter/rules/doc_page.rs) may also be a useful reference for implementing this.

Eventually, we'll want to apply this to all rules across all plugins, but that'll require the completion of #14743 first.

Plugins we can currently enforce this check on:

- `oxc`
- `import` (after #15171 is merged)
- `nextjs`
- `node`
- `react-perf`
- `vitest`
- `jsx-a11y` (after #15209 is merged)
oxc-project/oxc#15095.title: linter: Add test script to CI to enforce config option documentation for all rules

oxc-project/oxc#15101.body:
## Summary

Currently, `TSMappedType` uses `TSTypeParameter` to represent its type parameter, which includes several fields that are syntactically invalid in mapped types (`r#const`, `default`). We should replace `type_parameter` with dedicated fields that accurately represent the allowed syntax and match the TSESTree specification.

## Current Implementation

```rust
pub struct TSMappedType<'a> {
    pub span: Span,
    pub type_parameter: Box<'a, TSTypeParameter<'a>>,
    pub name_type: Option<TSType<'a>>,
    pub type_annotation: Option<TSType<'a>>,
    pub optional: Option<TSMappedTypeModifierOperator>,
    pub readonly: Option<TSMappedTypeModifierOperator>,
    pub scope_id: Cell<Option<ScopeId>>,
}
```

## Problem

1. **Semantic Mismatch**: `TSTypeParameter` includes fields that are not valid in mapped type syntax:
   - `r#const` - mapped types cannot use `const` modifier
   - `default` - mapped types cannot have default types
   - `in` - variance annotation not applicable to mapped types
   - `out` - variance annotation not applicable to mapped types

2. **TSESTree Incompatibility**: The current structure doesn't match the TSESTree specification, which defines mapped types with separate `key` and `constraint` fields rather than a full type parameter.

This creates both a semantic mismatch where the AST structure allows syntactically invalid constructs and a compatibility issue with TypeScript tooling.

## Proposed Solution

Replace `type_parameter: Box<'a, TSTypeParameter<'a>>` with two explicit fields to match TSESTree:

````rust
#[ast(visit)]
#[scope]
#[derive(Debug)]
#[generate_derive(CloneIn, Dummy, TakeIn, GetSpan, GetSpanMut, ContentEq, ESTree)]
pub struct TSMappedType<'a> {
    pub span: Span,
    /// The type parameter name (e.g., `P` in `[P in keyof T]`)
    pub key: BindingIdentifier<'a>,
    /// The constraint (e.g., `keyof T` in `[P in keyof T]`)
    pub constraint: TSType<'a>,
    pub name_type: Option<TSType<'a>>,
    pub type_annotation: Option<TSType<'a>>,
    pub optional: Option<TSMappedTypeModifierOperator>,
    pub readonly: Option<TSMappedTypeModifierOperator>,
    pub scope_id: Cell<Option<ScopeId>>,
}
````

Note: The existing `#[estree(add_fields(key = TSMappedTypeKey, constraint = TSMappedTypeConstraint))]` can be removed as these will now be real fields.

## Benefits

1. **Type Safety**: The AST structure will only allow syntactically valid constructs
2. **TSESTree Compatibility**: Matches the structure expected by TypeScript tooling and TypeScript-ESLint
3. **Clarity**: The intent and constraints are explicit in the type definition
4. **Maintainability**: Reduces confusion about which `TSTypeParameter` fields are applicable
5. **Correctness**: Prevents the possibility of creating invalid AST nodes

## Example

For the mapped type:
```ts
type Readonly<T> = { readonly [P in keyof T]: T[P] }
```

- `key` would be the identifier `P` (as `BindingIdentifier`)
- `constraint` would be the type `keyof T` (as `TSType`)


## TSESTree Compatibility

This change aligns Oxc's AST with [typescript-eslint's TSESTree specification](https://github.com/typescript-eslint/typescript-eslint/blob/55ca033ee88cc95cfac4ad6dea2257fbeb1d4657/packages/ast-spec/src/type/TSMappedType/spec.ts), which defines `TSMappedType` with `key` and `constraint` fields. This could help us to remove specific converting logic from OXC AST to ESTree
oxc-project/oxc#15101.title: Replace `TSMappedType::type_parameter` with dedicated fields to match TSESTree

oxc-project/oxc#15119.body:
* add --lsp to both oxlint and oxfmt binary https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/pull/15038
* `oxlint --lsp` will only provide linting and `oxfmt --lsp` will only provide formatting
refactor oxc_language_server to make linting and formatting capabilities pluggable to support above
* zed / jetbrain / vim (coc) will call these servers separately
* for vscode extension, align to the above editors, i.e. no longer bundle its own oxlint / oxfmt version, it will always call the servers from project
* for releasing versions, oxlint and oxfmt will be published at the same time with the exact same version, but people can bump packages individually to avoid chaos.

---

Change in architecture:

* `oxc_language_server` should accept different tools
* `oxlint --lsp` and `oxfmt --lsp` uses `oxc_language_server` with only the required tool
* IDE is calling `oxlint --lsp` and `oxfmt --lsp`
* `oxc_language_server` should be generic to support other tools too (vitest, ...)
oxc-project/oxc#15119.title: language_server: rearchitect lsp for oxlint, oxfmt and vscode

oxc-project/oxc#15247.body: An issue for dumping various ideas on improvements to the `.oxlinrc.json` [configuration_schema.json](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/main/npm/oxlint/configuration_schema.json). These sections can be split into individual issues based on whether the maintainers think they're worthwhile improvements. Basically, I think it's a _much_ better developer experience if their editor (VS Code, Cursor, IntelliJ, etc) can provide useful information/links about the configuration options and do some basic validation. This is already implemented in many regards, but it's imperfect right now. ## Add `markdownDescription` for everything that has `description` [VS Code (and thus Cursor and friends) support this for JSON Schemas](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/json#_use-rich-formatting-in-hovers) Unfortunately there's no current way to do this in the schemars crate as far as I know. But schematic has code already that hacks this into schemars, and they also use schemars v0.8, so it can potentially be reused by oxc?: https://github.com/moonrepo/schematic/blob/d3a9422cead2802b940c96f8ab8b188c86ab281f/crates/schematic/src/schema/renderers/json_schema.rs#L85 The difference in quality of hover info is pretty obvious, before and after using `markdownDescription` with VS Code: We still want to keep the `description` field on all schema elements that have it for editors/other tools which won't recognize `markdownDescription`. The `markdownDescription` will always have the same value as the `description` field, at least for now. In the future we could maybe strip markdown somehow, but not worth the trouble for now. ## Generate the configuration schema with embedded documentation for each rule This may be difficult, but it seems possible? Basically, when I hover over a rule, it should be able to display the docs defined from the `declare_oxc_lint!` macro. Or at the very least, it should link to the documentation page for each rule. This would only support the built-in rules, no js plugin rules obviously. Right now it just displays this for every rule: ## Improved validation/enforcement of valid values I think this was better in previous versions, but there have been regressions in the validation with the emitted json schema See this comment for an example: https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/pull/12117#issuecomment-3483329249 Basically, we should be able to have validation in-editor like this: ## Add tests for the configuration_schema.json to ensure regressions do not occur Moved this section to its own issue in #15468 To ensure the validations don't break in the future, we may want to consider a simple CI script to validate example JSON files that _should_ fail to validate? e.g. an `.oxlintrc.json` file with an invalid `plugins` value or invalid `categories` value. Not everything can be validated, such as the `jsPlugins` array, but some parts can be, and we should ensure we keep it working for end-users via some basic tests. We could potentially run these tests via JavaScript with [ajv](https://ajv.js.org/)? Although there is presumably an equivalent for Rust we could use.
oxc-project/oxc#15247.title: linter: .oxlintrc.json configuration file improvements

esbuild lowers this case (https://github.com/evanw/esbuild/commit/58ac420581421b36c03649c0018286e5a8e58ffd#diff-06572a96a58dc510037d5efa622f9bec8519bc1beab13c9f251e97e657a9d4edL48-R50, [esbuild try](https://esbuild.github.io/try/#dAAwLjI1LjEyAABmb29gPC9zY3JpcHQ+YA)) so the user doesn't have to configure it.

oxc-project/oxc#15383.body:
## Bug Description

The `no_var` rule's autofix is producing invalid syntax when converting an uninitialized `var` declaration to `const`.

## Location

`crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/eslint/no_var.rs`

## Details

**Input code:**
```javascript
var foo
```

**Fixed code (invalid):**
```javascript
const foo
```

**Parse error:**
```
Missing initializer in const declaration at offset 6
Help: Add an initializer (e.g. ` = undefined`) here
```

## Root Cause

The fixer replaces `var` with `const` without checking if the variable is initialized. `const` declarations require an initializer, but `var` declarations do not.

## Expected Behavior

The fixer should:
1. Convert uninitialized `var` to `let` (not `const`)
2. Only convert to `const` if the variable is initialized and never reassigned

## Test Path

`crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/eslint/no_var.rs` - test `no_var::test`

Relates to #14833
oxc-project/oxc#15383.title: Linter fixer bug: no_var rule converts uninitialized var to const without initializer

oxc-project/oxc#15384.body:
## Bug Description

The `forward_ref_uses_ref` rule's autofix is producing invalid syntax when removing a `forwardRef` wrapper, leaving behind an anonymous function declaration.

## Location

`crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/react/forward_ref_uses_ref.rs`

## Details

**Input code:**
```javascript
forwardRef(function (a) {})
```

**Fixed code (invalid):**
```javascript
function (a) {}
```

**Parse error:**
```
Expected function name at offset 9
Help: Function name is required in function declaration or named export
```

## Root Cause

The fixer removes the `forwardRef()` call wrapper but leaves a function declaration without a name. Anonymous function expressions are only valid in expression contexts, not as statements.

## Expected Behavior

The fixer should either:
1. Not attempt to fix if it would result in an anonymous function declaration
2. Convert to a function expression (wrap in parentheses or assign to a variable)
3. Preserve any existing variable assignment context

## Test Path

`crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/react/forward_ref_uses_ref.rs` - test `forward_ref_uses_ref::test`

Relates to #14833
oxc-project/oxc#15384.title: Linter fixer bug: forward_ref_uses_ref produces invalid function declaration

oxc-project/oxc#15413.body:
### What version of Oxlint are you using?

1.26.0

### What command did you run?

`npm run oxlint`

### What does your `.oxlintrc.json` config file look like?

```jsonc
{
  "jsPlugins": ["jsdoc"]
}
```


### What happened?

Error message is returned, `Plugin name 'jsdoc' is reserved, and cannot be used for JS plugins`.

It would be good if we updated the user experience when hitting reserved names:

- Improve this error message (or linked to the docs maybe?), to explain that `jsdoc` rules are already implemented via a native implementation in Rust, and oxlint cannot support it as a jsPlugin because it wouldn't know whether to pull rules you've configured from the native implementation or from JS.
- Add the list of disallowed names to the JSON Schema, so that VS Code/other editors would show a warning in .oxlintrc.json if you put any of the disallowed values into the `jsPlugins` array.
oxc-project/oxc#15413.title: linter(plugins): Improve the error message/config schema for "reserved" plugin names

oxc-project/oxc#15503.body:
### What version of Oxlint are you using?

1.25.0

### What command did you run?

npx oxlint

### What does your `.oxlintrc.json` config file look like?

```jsonc
{
	"categories": {
		"restriction": "deny"
	}
}
```


### What happened?

`oxc` incorrectly emits

> Unexpected var, use let or const instead.
help: Replace var with let or const

oxc [eslint(no-var)](https://oxc.rs/docs/guide/usage/linter/rules/eslint/no-var.html)

for a `declare global` block in TypeScript code, like

```ts
declare global {
	var __SPECIAL_VALUE__: boolean | undefined;
}
```

If we update that code to

```ts
declare global {
	const __SPECIAL_VALUE__: boolean | undefined;
}
```

and try accessing

```ts
globalThis.__SPECIAL_VALUE__
```

later, we get

```
Property '__SPECIAL_VALUE__' does not exist on type 'typeof globalThis'.ts(2339)
```
oxc-project/oxc#15503.title: linter: `oxc` incorrectly emits oxc(eslint(no-var)) for `declare global` block in TypeScript code

https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/ecdda9e66a08310f95eae623583560ee0485a5a7/crates/oxc_parser/src/lib.rs#L481-L487

oxc-project/oxc#15555.body: Currently, the `oxfmt` will print out all file paths with some info. I tested Biome and Prettier; neither of these has the same behavior, and it's also annoying because it doesn't provide any help. `--check` and `--list-different` already support printing difference files. Additionally, since the `oxfmt` is blazing fast, the different behavior causes our formatter benchmark to be slightly slower than expected.
oxc-project/oxc#15555.title: oxfmt: don't show the formatted file path in write mode

oxc-project/oxc#15564.body:
Currently `ast_tools` (our codegen) depends on versions of Oxc crates (e.g. `oxc_parser`) from crates.io, instead of the local repo.

https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/bb5f8ca3bb88f778bf2183685a8a0dbe73ed0bdb/tasks/ast_tools/Cargo.toml#L16-L28 

### Why?

When you change an AST type (e.g. add a field to one of the structs), you need to run `ast_tools` codegen to update traits and various other code to match that change.

Until you do that, crates like `oxc_parser` will not compile.

Therefore, the codegen itself cannot depend on local copy of these crates, as then the codegen won't compile either. But the only way to get the codegen to compile... is to run the codegen. Catch 22!

### Why is this a problem?

Having multiple versions of our own crates in `Cargo.lock` complicates dependency management.

### Solution

How `ast_tools` is structured:

* The codegen contains separate generators for Rust code and JS code.
* Only the generators that produce JS code rely on `oxc_*` crates.

Therefore, we can fix this as follows:

* Add Cargo feature `generate-js` to `ast_tools` which enables JS generators.
* Make all `oxc_*` crate dependencies `optional`, only enabled when `generate-js` feature is enabled.
* `generate-js` feature enabled by default.
* Alter `just ast` command to:

```sh
cargo run -p oxc_ast_tools || {
  cargo run -p oxc_ast_tools --no-default-features
  cargo run -p oxc_ast_tools
}
```

If the first run fails due to compilation failure (the catch 22 problem), then run again with dependencies on `oxc_*` crates disabled. This will update the Rust code so that `oxc_*` crates will now compile. Then run it again - it should compile with the `oxc_*` crates this time, and generate the JS files too.
oxc-project/oxc#15564.title: Remove dependency of `ast_tools` on Oxc crates from crates.io

oxc-project/oxc#15576.body:
The naming of the exposed functions are inconsistent. The current namings are:
- `oxc-minify`
  - `minify` (sync)
- `oxc-parser`
  - `parseSync` (sync)
  - `parseAsync` (async)
- `oxc-transform`
  - `transform` (sync)
  - `transformAsync` (async)
  - `isolatedDeclaration` (sync)

We should change to our esbuild names them:
- `*` (async) + `*Sync`

for consistency.

- [ ] document that the async versions will spawn a thread which can be slow, ymmv.
- [ ] update all the benchmarks for these functions
oxc-project/oxc#15576.title: napi: align API signature

oxc-project/oxc#15588.body:
It is common for formatters like Prettier to automatically add trailing commas to json/jsonc files.

The current schema does not allow for trailing commas, resulting in text editors linting it as an error.
https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/main/npm/oxfmt/configuration_schema.json

Can modify the schema with an additional property to allow for trailing commas

```jsonc
{
  "allowTrailingCommas": true,
}
```
oxc-project/oxc#15588.title: oxfmt: allow trailing commas in `.oxfmtrc.jsonc`

oxc-project/oxc#15615.body:
Currently, the `jsx-key` rule does not support configuration options from jsx-key, which results in lint failures due to some config options defaulting to different values than what the oxlint rule supports.

See https://github.com/jsx-eslint/eslint-plugin-react/blob/f2869fd6dc76ceb863c5e2aeea8bf4d392508775/docs/rules/jsx-key.md

oxlint rule source: https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/78c38978b7b8137d7910e8679815bc32e008b4f5/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/react/jsx_key.rs

The missing config options are these, they all default to `false`:
- `checkFragmentShorthand`
- `checkKeyMustBeforeSpread`
- `warnOnDuplicates`

These are necessary to easily migrate from the eslint react plugin to oxlint.
oxc-project/oxc#15615.title: linter: Add missing config options in react/jsx-key rule

oxc-project/oxc#15628.title: tasks/common: Find and use more real-world sources for formatter benchmark

oxc-project/oxc#15630.body:
This issue is not actionable until rule options are implemented (#14825), but am opening issue now so we don't forget.

### The feature

`rule.meta.defaultOptions` defined by the rule are merged into options provided by user in their config.

https://eslint.org/docs/latest/extend/custom-rules#option-defaults

https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/88789019ea9cbda70668894694b254b4ad4c9230/apps/oxlint/src-js/plugins/rule_meta.ts#L42-L48

### Impact of lacking this feature

Medium impact.

Absence of support will:

* Likely prevent some ESLint rules working, or
* Force user to manually provide all required options - which would be unexpected and annoying, but a viable workaround.

### Implementation

* Rust calls into JS to load a plugin (`loadPlugin`). JS would need to return `defaultOptions` for each rule back to Rust (as JSON).
* Perform options merging on Rust side.

I don't believe options for rules are merged between 2 different configs (via `extends` or `overrides`), so merging `defaultOptions` into provided options could happen per config, at config-parsing time. The options sent back back to JS (see #14825) would be the options *after* merging in `defaultOptions`.
oxc-project/oxc#15630.title: Linter plugins: Support `rule.meta.defaultOptions`

oxc-project/oxc#15631.body:
This issue is not actionable until rule options are implemented (#14825), but am opening issue now so we don't forget.

It's quite low priority (see "Impact" section below).

### The feature

`rule.meta.schema` defines the shape of options that a rule accepts. Oxlint should check options provided by user in their config against this schema.

https://eslint.org/docs/latest/extend/custom-rules#options-schemas

https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/88789019ea9cbda70668894694b254b4ad4c9230/apps/oxlint/src-js/plugins/rule_meta.ts#L37-L41

Note: It's unclear to me if validation against the schema should happen *before*, or *after* `defaultOptions` is merged into user-provided options (#15630).

### Impact of lacking this feature

Low impact.

Lacking schema validation will not prevent any ESLint rules from running correctly, but just results in user potentially getting an unhelpful error message e.g. "Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'someProperty')" instead of more useful e.g. "`options.blah` must be an object".

### Implementation

I don't believe options for rules are merged between 2 different configs (via `extends` or `overrides`), so validation could happen once during config parsing, not for every file being linted.

Probably not too hard to implement with AI, since it's based around standard JSON schema validation. I imagine there's a Rust crate for this which integrates with `serde`.
oxc-project/oxc#15631.title: Linter plugins: Validate user-provided options against `rule.meta.schema`

### What happened?

JSON is no longer printed due to the changes in #15213.

Previously, in 1.28.0, you'd get an output like this:
```
  {
    "scope": "vue",
    "value": "require-default-export",
    "category": "suspicious"
  },
  {
    "scope": "vue",
    "value": "require-typed-ref",
    "category": "style"
  },
  {
    "scope": "vue",
    "value": "valid-define-emits",
    "category": "correctness"
  },
  {
    "scope": "vue",
    "value": "valid-define-props",
    "category": "correctness"
  }
```

Apparently the tests don't test this CLI behavior directly and so didn't catch this regression. I only noticed it while messing with the CLI for unrelated things.

oxc-project/oxc#15849.body:
Call `prettier` and get its config, then pick out the options we use or differ, then save to `.oxfmtrc.jsonc`

### Migration

- If `printWidth` specified, use it as-is.
  - Otherwise, set `printWidth: 80` for prettier's default
- Warn or comment out not supported format options like `experimentalXxx`
- Warn *meta-transform* plugins may not be applied?
- Anything else?

### Consideration

- First, we need to understand `prettier` API for config resolution
  - IIRC, `prettier.resolveConfig()` requires file path, because their config is resolved by each target file
  - In that case, which file path to be targeted?
  - We do not support nested config, always target root(cwd) config?

I was trying out oxfmt on an Angular project and found that comments are moved incorrectly when placed in decorators.

I am using oxfmt 0.14 through pnpx.

Before:
```ts
// test.ts
import { Component } from "@angular/core";

@Component({
  selector: "my-component", // test
})
export class AppMyComponent {}
```

After `pnpx oxfmt ./test.ts` (no config file):
```ts
// test.ts
import { Component } from "@angular/core"; // test

@Component({
  selector: "my-component",
})
export class AppMyComponent {}
```
oxc-project/oxc#15860.title: formatter: Comments in class decorator properties are moved to imports

oxc-project/oxc#15863.body:
When using JS-based plugins **and** the import plugin in oxc, linting a very large number of files can lead to system resource exhaustion under certain workloads. To prevent this from occusing, oxlint returns an pre-emptivly returns an error in this scenario.

🔧 Workaround until full solution

The recommended solution at this time, is to split your lint configuration into two separate `.oxlintrc.json` files:
1. One configuration dedicated to JS plugins only
2. Another configuration purely for the import plugin

Then, run the linter in two passes:
1. Run the linter with the first config (JS plugins only)
2. Run the linter with the second config (native rules, including `import` plugin)

By splitting the workload, it reduces the concurrent resource demands and avoids the resource exhaustion scenario.

🔧 What we're doing now?

We appreciate that this is not an ideal solution for many users. We are working to refactor our architecture and implementation in order to fix these scenarios however this is non-trivial and will take time to implement a full, robust and complete solution.
oxc-project/oxc#15863.title: linter: Resource exhaustion when combining JS-plugins + import plugin

```
export export function foo() {
}
```

Reports:

```
  x TS(1029): 'export' modifier must precede 'declare' modifier.
   ,-[:1:8]
```

oxc-project/oxc#15927.body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/discussions/15918
Originally posted by **htunnicliff** November 21, 2025 I'd like to propose that the issue descriptions managed by CI (e.g. #1170) receive a few updates to improve readability: - [x] Replace ✨ with ✅ - A check mark is more readily associated with something being finished or done - The yellow is harder to see against a bright background - [x] Sort the rules in each table in alphabetical order - [x] Make the name of each rule a hyperlink to that rule's docs - The full links in the "Docs" column take up a lot of space - [x] Add a column "Rationale" that provides an explanation for why an unimplemented rule (🚫) was not implemented - These would need to be filled in manually, but it could be really helpful to have this - [ ] Add a link to the oxc docs for each implemented rule? (may be a bit difficult for alias rules like those in vitest) What do maintainers think about this? I'd be happy to make the necessary PRs if these changes are welcome!
oxc-project/oxc#15927.title: Improving the readability of automated plugin issues

oxc-project/oxc#15977.body:
Update the scripts we use to generate the rule tables in GitHub to also track rules with `pending` fixes in some form.

This would allow us to more easily track which rules still need to have auto-fixes created for them. We can add this to the existing tables or make a separate issue for tracking these. 

Rules which are not explicitly marked as pending in the `declare_oxc_lint!` macro should be ignored when checking for pending fixes.
oxc-project/oxc#15977.title: linter: Update automated issues for linter rule ports to also track pending fixes

oxc-project/oxc#16099.body:
Oxc supports `armv7-unknown-linux-gnueabihf` target, and we intend to continue support for it (#16094).

This platform is 32-bit.

If we are officially supporting 32-bit, we should run tests on a 32-bit arch in CI. Similar to what we do for big-endian, that job could run only on main, not on every PR.

Note: WASM is also 32-bit, but there are some slight differences in memory layouts between WASM32 and some other 32-bit archs, which can affect the size of types. Some of our code contains assertions about size of types, and these can cause compilation failure on 32-bit systems which we won't notice unless we test them.

* #13694
* #15984

Aside from type sizes, there may also be further subtle differences that I'm not aware of.

It'd be ideal to test on the actual arch we support `armv7-unknown-linux-gnueabihf` (or emulation of it), rather than assuming that all 32-bit archs behave the same. They may not.
oxc-project/oxc#16099.title: Run tests in CI on 32-bit arch

oxc-project/oxc#16136.body:
input
```ts
type CamelToSnakeCase<TCamelCaseString extends string> =
  TCamelCaseString extends `${infer TStringConvertedSoFar}${infer TStringYetToConvert}`
    ? `${TStringConvertedSoFar extends Capitalize<TStringConvertedSoFar>
        ? '_'
        : ''}${Lowercase<TStringConvertedSoFar>}${CamelToSnakeCase<TStringYetToConvert>}`
    : TCamelCaseString;
```

prettier 3.6.2
```ts
type CamelToSnakeCase<TCamelCaseString extends string> =
  TCamelCaseString extends `${infer TStringConvertedSoFar}${infer TStringYetToConvert}`
    ? `${TStringConvertedSoFar extends Capitalize<TStringConvertedSoFar>
        ? '_'
        : ''}${Lowercase<TStringConvertedSoFar>}${CamelToSnakeCase<TStringYetToConvert>}`
    : TCamelCaseString;
```

oxfmt 0.15
```ts
type CamelToSnakeCase<TCamelCaseString extends string> =
  TCamelCaseString extends `${infer TStringConvertedSoFar}${infer TStringYetToConvert}`
    ? `${
        TStringConvertedSoFar extends Capitalize<TStringConvertedSoFar> ? '_' : ''
      }${Lowercase<TStringConvertedSoFar>}${CamelToSnakeCase<TStringYetToConvert>}`
    : TCamelCaseString;
```

config

```json
{
  "printWidth": 100,
  "tabWidth": 2,
  "useTabs": false,
  "semi": true,
  "singleQuote": true,
  "quoteProps": "as-needed",
  "jsxSingleQuote": false,
  "trailingComma": "all",
  "bracketSpacing": true,
  "bracketSameLine": false,
  "arrowParens": "always"
}
```

oxc-project/oxc#16247.body:
Prettier does have a `consistent` option on `quoteProps` so when an object has properties that require quotes, it will add quotes to *all* properties on the object for consistent format and look. Currently, oxfmt (just like Biome) does not have this option.

https://prettier.io/docs/options#quote-props

This is very useful when working with i18n data, where some properties can be strings with spaces that require quotes, while others are single words. Currently, oxfmt's default `as-needed` mixes the formatting, which looks ugly.
oxc-project/oxc#16247.title: formatter: Support `quoteProps: "consistent"` option

oxc-project/oxc#16263.body:
### What version of Oxlint are you using?

1.30.0

### What command did you run?

oxlint --type-aware src/**/*.{js,ts}

### What does your `.oxlintrc.json` config file look like?

```jsonc
{
  "$schema": "./node_modules/oxlint/configuration_schema.json",
  "ignorePatterns": ["/dist", "/node_modules"],
  "plugins": ["eslint", "oxc", "typescript", "import", "promise", "unicorn"],
  "categories": {
    "correctness": "error",
    "nursery": "error",
    "pedantic": "error",
    "perf": "error",
    "restriction": "error",
    "style": "error",
    "suspicious": "error"
  }
}

```

### What happened?

https://typescript-eslint.io/rules/switch-exhaustiveness-check/

In typescript-eslint, option `allowDefaultCaseForExhaustiveSwitch` is true

In oxlint, option `allowDefaultCaseForExhaustiveSwitch` is false

This result in errors in oxlint that don't show up with typescript-eslint
oxc-project/oxc#16263.title: linter: typescript/switch-exhaustiveness-check allowDefaultCaseForExhaustiveSwitch differs from typescript-eslint

oxc-project/oxc#16304.body:
e.g. the [two deprecated rules from eslint-plugin-n](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/issues/493), 3 from [jsx-a11y](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/issues/1141), [the 3 from typescript](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/issues/2180), etc.

I don't see a strong reason to support the deprecated rules without good reason, so we should consider updating the list in [oxlint-rules.mjs](https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/523a30a0164932029e977090d441041fff13c735/tasks/lint_rules/src/oxlint-rules.mjs) to mark them as no-need-to-support.

I've done some of this work in #16305.
oxc-project/oxc#16304.title: linter: Consider marking all deprecated rules from ESLint plugins as won't-support

oxc-project/oxc#16321.body:
Currently when I run something like this, I just get `--write is not expected in this context`:

```
connorshea@mac oxc-project.github.io % pnpm oxfmt --write
Error: --write is not expected in this context
```

So if I just try to replace all my existing `prettier` commands in my repo, and one of them is a command to format all my files, I'll end up with a vague error message.

It'd be great if we added `--write` as a simple command that:

- Only exists to print a message that says "Error: The `--write` flag is not necessary for oxfmt, oxfmt will format files for you by default."
- Does not show up in `--help`
oxc-project/oxc#16321.title: oxfmt: Implement `--write` as a "secret" command that just explains that `--write` is not necessary

oxc-project/oxc#16330.body:
Currently, we only support in-editor suggestions, we should also support fixes from the CLI.

The flow that should look like:
1. oxlint rule run
2. oxlint fixes applied
3. tsgolint rule run
4. tsgolint fixes applied.

Note: this fixes being applied should be applied on the oxlint side, **not** on the tsgolint side
oxc-project/oxc#16330.title: linter: support `--fix` for type aware rules

```diff
-          .TraceEntryPointsPlugin as typeof import('./webpack/plugins/next-trace-entrypoints-plugin').TraceEntryPointsPlugin)(
-          {
-            rootDir: dir,
-            appDir: appDir,
-            pagesDir: pagesDir,
-            esmExternals: config.experimental.esmExternals,
-            outputFileTracingRoot: config.outputFileTracingRoot,
-            appDirEnabled: hasAppDir,
-            traceIgnores: [],
-            compilerType,
-          }
-        ),
+        new (
+          (
+            require('./webpack/plugins/next-trace-entrypoints-plugin') as typeof import('./webpack/plugins/next-trace-entrypoints-plugin')
+          )
+            .TraceEntryPointsPlugin as typeof import('./webpack/plugins/next-trace-entrypoints-plugin').TraceEntryPointsPlugin
+        )({
+          rootDir: dir,
+          appDir: appDir,
+          pagesDir: pagesDir,
+          esmExternals: config.experimental.esmExternals,
+          outputFileTracingRoot: config.outputFileTracingRoot,
+          appDirEnabled: hasAppDir,
+          traceIgnores: [],
+          compilerType,
+        }),
       // Moment.js is an extremely popular library that bundles large locale files
       // by default due to how Webpack interprets its code. This is a practical
       // solution that requires the user to opt into importing specific locales.
```

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/bfb4a8c9526f6be84c2584d8ab608b37bb5c4a5d/packages/next/src/build/webpack-config.ts#L2044

oxc-project/oxc#16356.body:
I've labelled this as a bug, though I'm not sure if it actually is. But to my mind, it seems strange.

In a project with only 1 config file, `ConfigStoreBuilder::from_oxlintrc` gets called twice.

### To reproduce

Add to top of `from_oxlintrc` method:

```rs
eprintln!("> from_oxlintrc");
```

Run `oxlint` tests with snapshot updates enabled:

```sh
cd apps/oxlint; pnpm run build-test && pnpm test -u; cd ../..
```

Each snapshot has 2 lines `> from_oxlintrc`.

The only exceptions are fixtures where there's an early error e.g. `fixtures/import_error`, where there's only 1 call.

When there's a nested config, e.g. `fixtures/nested_config`, there's 3 calls (not 4, so it's not calling twice for each config).

It's not related to JS plugins - 2 x calls in `fixtures/built_in_no_errors`, which doesn't use JS plugins.

### Expected behavior?

If this is intentional, we'll need to change how options for JS plugins are stored. Currently they're stored twice each, which is wasteful.
oxc-project/oxc#16356.title: Linter: `ConfigStoreBuilder::from_oxlintrc` called twice

oxc-project/oxc#16398.body:
Rule options (defined in configs) are stored as `Option<Vec<serde_json::Value>>`.

* `None` = No options.
* `Some` = 1 or more options.

Note: `Some` containing an empty `Vec` is illegal (though that's not enforced by type system, which is unfortunate).

https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/3f3d646280340bd4851e74297dca6d6a68b8466b/crates/oxc_linter/src/config/rules.rs#L50-L61

It's rare for a rule to have multiple options, so `config` field is very commonly `None` or `Some` containing a `Vec` with a single entry. We can optimize for the common cases by using `SmallVec` instead (from [smallvec](https://docs.rs/smallvec) crate).

```diff
-   pub config: Option<Vec<serde_json::Value>>,
+   pub config: SmallVec<[serde_json::Value; 1]>,
```

This would reduce allocations.

Note: I suggest we also remove the `Option` for 2 reasons:

1. It makes it impossible to have `Some` containing an empty `Vec` - more type safe.

2. The one advantage that `Option<Vec>` has is that creating `Option::None` is only 1 x `usize` memory write, whereas creating an empty `Vec` is 3 x `usize` writes. But this isn't true with `SmallVec` - creating an empty `SmallVec` is also only 1 x `usize` write. So once we switch to `SmallVec`, the advantage of the `Option` disappears.
oxc-project/oxc#16398.title: Linter: Use `SmallVec` for storing rule options

oxc-project/oxc#16491.body:
### What version of Oxlint are you using?

1.31.0

### What command did you run?

oxlint --type-aware

### What does your `.oxlintrc.json` config file look like?

```jsonc
{
  "plugins": [
    "typescript",
    "unicorn",
    "react",
    "react-perf",
    "oxc",
    "import",
    "promise",
    "vitest"
  ],
  "ignorePatterns": [
    "node_modules",
    "dist/**",
    "packages/protocol/dist/**",
    "packages/log-translator/dist/**"
  ],
  "rules": {
    "jest/expect-expect": [
      "warn",
      {
        "assertFunctionNames": [
          "expect",
          "test_text_with_page",
          "test_previous",
          "test_next",
          "test_pre_ellipsis",
          "test_post_ellipsis"
        ]
      }
    ],
    "jest/no-standalone-expect": [
      "error",
      {
        "additionalTestBlockFunctions": ["test.for"]
      }
    ],
    "typescript/unbound-method": "off",
    "typescript/no-floating-promises": "off",
    "typescript/no-redundant-type-constituents": "off"
  }
}
```


### What happened?

Good day,

I am using some helper test functions in my test like `test_previous` and `test_next` I was getting an error that **expect** is not in the test. I have realized that I need to tell the linter which of my helper functions have **expect** in them.

[Docs](https://oxc.rs/docs/guide/usage/linter/rules/jest/expect-expect.html) regarding this rule only mention _assertFunctionNamesJest_ and _assertFunctionNamesVitest_. I have tried adding those helper functions into _assertFunctionNamesJest_, but that didn't work. (**_Might even be a separate bug_**)

Solution was to use just _assertFunctionNames_ option. Which is not mentioned in the docs at all.
oxc-project/oxc#16491.title: linter: jest/expect-expect doesn't mention assertFunctionNames

### What version of Oxlint are you using? 1.31.0 ### What command did you run? `oxlint -c .oxintrc.json --format junit` ### What does your `.oxlintrc.json` config file look like? N/A ### What happened? consider this default oxlint output for a given two file project ``` > pnpm oxlint *.tsx -D all × eslint(curly): Expected { after 'if' condition. ╭─[bar.tsx:4:22] 3 │ const DummyComponent: React.FC = () => { 4 │ if (ref.current) ref.current.focus() · ─────────────────── 5 │ ╰──── help: Replace `ref.current.focus()` with `{ref.current.focus()}`. × eslint(curly): Expected { after 'if' condition. ╭─[foo.tsx:7:22] 6 │ useEffect(() => { 7 │ if (ref.current) ref.current.focus() · ─────────────────── 8 │ }, []) ╰──── help: Replace `ref.current.focus()` with `{ref.current.focus()}`. × eslint(no-constant-binary-expression): Unexpected constant nullishness on the left-hand side of a "??" expression ╭─[foo.tsx:11:11] 10 │ return ( 11 │ ╭─▶
{Boolean(ref.current) ?? ( 12 │ │ 13 │ ╰─▶ )} 14 │
╰──── help: This expression always evaluates to the constant on the left-hand side Found 0 warnings and 3 errors. Finished in 40ms on 2 files with 335 rules using 12 threads. ``` It has simillar correct outputs with other formatters
stylish ``` > pnpm oxlint *.tsx -D all --format stylish /tmp/tmp.lLodRLQny0/foo.tsx 7:22 error Expected { after 'if' condition. eslint(curly) 11:11 error Unexpected constant nullishness on the left-hand side of a "??" expression eslint(no-constant-binary-expression) /tmp/tmp.lLodRLQny0/bar.tsx 4:20 error Expected { after 'if' condition. eslint(curly) ```
Json ``` > pnpm oxlint *.tsx -D all --format json { "diagnostics": [{"message": "Expected { after 'if' condition.","code": "eslint(curly)","severity": "error","causes": [],"url": "https://oxc.rs/docs/guide/usage/linter/rules/eslint/curly.html","help": "Replace `ref.current.focus()` with `{ref.current.focus()}`.","filename": "foo.tsx","labels": [{"span": {"offset": 178,"length": 19,"line": 7,"column": 22}}],"related": []}, {"message": "Unexpected constant nullishness on the left-hand side of a \"??\" expression","code": "eslint(no-constant-binary-expression)","severity": "error","causes": [],"url": "https://oxc.rs/docs/guide/usage/linter/rules/eslint/no-constant-binary-expression.html","help": "This expression always evaluates to the constant on the left-hand side","filename": "foo.tsx","labels": [{"span": {"offset": 229,"length": 69,"line": 11,"column": 11}}],"related": []}, {"message": "Expected { after 'if' condition.","code": "eslint(curly)","severity": "error","causes": [],"url": "https://oxc.rs/docs/guide/usage/linter/rules/eslint/curly.html","help": "Replace `ref.current.focus()` with `{ref.current.focus()}`.","filename": "bar.tsx","labels": [{"span": {"offset": 94,"length": 19,"line": 4,"column": 20}}],"related": []}], "number_of_files": 2, "number_of_rules": 335, "threads_count": 12, "start_time": 0.029430435 } ```
Checkstyle ``` > pnpm oxlint *.tsx -D all --format checkstyle ```
However for the junit output - it only has a **singular** error, and for only a singular file ``` > pnpm oxlint *.tsx -D all --format junit line 4, column 20, Expected { after 'if' condition. ``` Where we would instead expect multiple testSuites and multiple `errors` I have a PR that might fix this which will raise shortly - but needs someone to check it :D

oxc-project/oxc#16608.body:
Apply `oxc_formatter` for embedded JS/TS part inside of Prettier's default supported files. So, `xxx` should be any of `html`, `vue`, `markdown` and `mdx`.

Things like:

- Format consistency
- `oxfmt-ignore` comment and `experimentalSortImports`

will be available.

### TODOs

#### Vue
- [x] vue: `<script>...</script>`
  - `babel` / `typescript` w/ `__embeddedInHtml`
  - No special handling
- [x] vue: `<script generic="...">`
  - `babel-ts` w/ `__isEmbeddedTypescriptGenericParameters`
  - Extract `type T<${value}> = any`
  - Force `'` for `StringLiteral` (`singleQuote: true` is not enough) by `__isInHtmlAttribute`
  - `shouldHugJsExpression` w/ `__onHtmlBindingRoot`?
- [x] vue: `v-slot="..."` / `#default="..."`
  - `babel(-ts)` w/ `__isVueBindings`
  - Extract `function _(${text}) {}`
  - Force `'` for `StringLiteral` (`singleQuote: true` is not enough) by `__isInHtmlAttribute`
- [x] vue: `v-for="... in ..."` (LHS)
  - `babel(-ts)` w/ `__isVueForBindingLeft`+`__isInHtmlAttribute`+`__embeddedInHtml`
  - Extract `function _(${left}) {}`
  - Add `(` and `)` if multiple params
  - Force `'` for `StringLiteral` (`singleQuote: true` is not enough) by `__isInHtmlAttribute`
- [x] Enable for `vue`
  - Blocked by js-in-vue
- [ ] vue: `v-for="... in ..."` (RHS)
  - `__(js|ts)_expression`(= babel(-ts):isExpr)
- [ ] vue: `v-on="..."` / `@click="..."` (EXPR)
  - `__(js|ts)_expression`(= babel(-ts):isExpr)
- [ ] vue: `v-on="..."` / `@click="..."` (STATEMENT)
  - `__vue_(ts_)event_binding`(= babel(-ts))
- [ ] vue: `{{ ... }}`
  - `__vue_(ts_)expression`(= babel(-ts):isExpr)
- [ ] vue: `v-bind="..."` / `:class="..."`
  - `__vue_(ts_)expression`(= babel(-ts):isExpr)
- [ ] vue: `v-if="..."`, `v-show="..."`, etc
  - `__(js/ts)_expression`(= babel(-ts):isExpr)

#### HTML

- [ ] html: `<script>...</script>`
  - `babel` / `typescript` w/ `__babelSourceType`+`__embeddedInHtml`
  - No special handling
- [ ] html: `onclick="..."`
  - `babel` w/ `__isHtmlInlineEventHandler`+`__isInHtmlAttribute`
  - Remove last `;` if single `ExpressionStatement`
  - Force `'` for `StringLiteral` (`singleQuote: true` is not enough) by `__isInHtmlAttribute`
- [ ] Enable for `html`
  - Blocked by js-in-html

#### Markdown/MDX

- [ ] markdown|mdx: code block
  - `inferParser()` -> `babel` / `typescript`
  - Remove first and last `;` if single `JSXElement` in `ExpressionStatement`
- [ ] mdx: import/export
  - `babel`
  - Do not support validation with [`__onHtmlBindingRoot`](https://github.com/prettier/prettier/blob/90983f40dce5e20beea4e5618b5e0426a6a7f4f0/src/language-markdown/embed.js#L71)
- [ ] mdx: jsx
  - `__(js|ts)_expression`(= babel(-ts):isExpr)
- [ ] Enable for `markdown` / `mdx`
  - Blocked by js(-in-(html|vue))-in-markdown

#### Svelte

- [ ] svelte: `<script>...</script>`
  - `typescript` / `babel-ts`
  - No special handling
- [ ] svelte: `{...}`
  - `svelteExpressionParser` / `svelteTSExpressionParser` w/ `_svelte_asFunction: undefined`
  - Extract `ExpressionStatement`: `(${statement}\n)`
- [ ] svelte: `{#snippet ...}`
  - `svelteExpressionParser` / `svelteTSExpressionParser` w/ `_svelte_asFunction: true`
  - Extract `FunctionDeclaration`: `function ${statement} {}`


#### Astro

- TODO: Research

### NOTE

- `to_prettier_doc.rs` implementation is not complete
  - There are still some difference pure `oxc_formatter` result vs `<script>` in vue result
  - Convert Prettier `Doc` -> `oxc_formatter` IR is not enough, need to handle Printer's behaviors
- tsx-in-vue is NOT supported
  - There is no signal to detect TSX or TS when called from Prettier
  - Only options is `maybeJSXRe.test(sourceText)`, but makes all ts-in-vue path slow
- Force single quote for `__isInHtmlAttribute` cases are not yet implemented
  - Just set `QuoteStyle::Single` for now
  - Strictly speaking, this is not enough since this options is just a preference
- js-in-angular is out of scope for now
  - May be possible to support `__ng_action`, `__ng_binding` and `__ng_interpolation`
  - But `__ng_directive` is not a valid JS/TS syntax at all
- `options.__embeddedInHtml` should be handled for html-in-js-in-html?
  - https://github.com/prettier/prettier/blob/6e85d4a7850f8a23c3c4b0030cc8cf94b5e503f7/src/language-js/embed/html.js#L61
- `options.__babelSourceType` should be handled for js-in-html?
  - https://github.com/prettier/prettier/blob/6e85d4a7850f8a23c3c4b0030cc8cf94b5e503f7/src/language-js/parse/babel.js#L123

```js
// @jsxRuntime classic
console.log(<></>) // @jsxFrag `
```
is transformed to
```js
// @jsxRuntime classic
console.log(/* @__PURE__ */ React.createElement(`, null));
```
. But this should be
```js
// @jsxRuntime classic
console.log(/* @__PURE__ */ React.createElement(React.Fragment, null));
```
(the pragma comment should be ignored).

Probably a warning should be output like esbuild does ([esbuild try](https://esbuild.github.io/try/#dAAwLjI3LjEALS1sb2FkZXI9anN4AC8vIEBqc3hSdW50aW1lIGNsYXNzaWMKY29uc29sZS5sb2coPD48Lz4pIC8vIEBqc3hGcmFnIGA)).

oxc-project/oxc#16763.body:
TL;DR: How do we enable embedded-language-formatting in non-JS files but keep it disabled in JS files?

---

Currently, the `embeddedLanguageFormatting` option is `off` by default.

This is because our embedded formatting for JS/TS files is not fully implemented. See #15180.

But at the same time, we now support non-JS/TS files, and embedded formatting for them is handled by Prettier, so it should work.

Because of the default `off`, a few issues have already been reported:

- #16673
- #16702
- #16945
- #17062
- https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/issues/15899#issuecomment-3667306577

I think we should change the default to `auto` now.
But I also prefer to keep `off` for JS/TS files for a while.

So..., how about 

- adding another flag `experimentalJsEmbeddedLanguageFormatting` to disable embedded formatting only for JS/TS files?
- Or add our own variant on `embeddedLanguageFormatting: auto | off | disble-oxc`?
- Or should we just disable the feature itself until we have finished with the JS/TS files?
  - I now tend to this
- Or just change default to `auto`
  - incomplete but works
oxc-project/oxc#16763.title: oxfmt: Change to `embeddedLanguageFormatting: "auto"` by default aligning with Prettier

oxc-project/oxc#16788.body:
### What version of Oxlint are you using?

latest

### What command did you run?

N/A

### What does your `.oxlintrc.json` config file look like?

N/A

### What happened?

Using this template because the bug report template said "If you are reporting a bug for Oxlint or the Oxc VS Code extension" but it doesn't seem the best fit. Changing the title to start with `vscode:` like I have seen on other issues though just in case. I am on version `1.32.0` of the vs code extension.

Given a scenario like mine where I have a `backend` and a `frontend` folder at the root (the frontend folder being basically a root for a typical frontend where my oxc configs and node modules live), there are enough settings to set and point the oxc plugin features to the right paths (although it seems a "oxc root dir" path setting would knock all of these out in my specific case). However there is a setting to turn on type aware linting, but not a setting for the path to the `oxlint-tsgolint` package, so my editor keeps reporting: 
```
WARN  oxc_language_server::linter::isolated_lint_handler] Failed to initialize type-aware linting: Failed to find tsgolint executable. You may need to add the `oxlint-tsgolint` package to your project?
``` 

Here is a snippet from my settings for the workspace with an example of what I need
```
    "oxc.path.oxfmt": "frontend/node_modules/.bin/oxfmt",
    "oxc.path.oxlint": "frontend/node_modules/.bin/oxlint",
    "oxc.tsConfigPath": "frontend/tsconfig.json",
    "oxc.configPath": "frontend/.oxlintrc.json",
    "oxc.typeAware": true,
    "oxc.oxlint-tsgolintPath": "frontend/node_modules/.bin/tsgolint" //  <-------- Need this setting -------------
```

Could this setting be added? I would be happy to tackle this in a PR if it is not already in the works.

Thanks!
oxc-project/oxc#16788.title: vscode: no configuration/setting for oxlint-tsgolint path

oxc-project/oxc#16845.body: In `crates/oxc_linter/src/tester.rs`, we should update the logic so that the tests for a given rule are _all_ run. Currently, it is set up to fail fast, and so the developer only gets to see the first test that fails. It'd be better if it would run all the tests so the developer can see _all_ failures on a given oxlint rule.
oxc-project/oxc#16845.title: linter: Run all test cases with Tester rather than failing fast

oxc-project/oxc#16862.body:
Many of the failures in the plugin conformance snapshot appear to be due to the `impliedStrict: true` option being set:

For example,

#### strict > valid

```js
function foo() { return; }
```

```json
{
  "languageOptions": {
    "parserOptions": {
      "ecmaFeatures": {
        "impliedStrict": true
      }
    }
  }
}
```

```
AssertionError [ERR_ASSERTION]: Should have no errors but had 1: [
  {
    ruleId: 'rule-to-test/strict',
    message: "Use the function form of 'use strict'.",
    messageId: 'function',
    severity: 1,
    nodeType: 'FunctionDeclaration',
    line: 1,
    column: 0,
    endLine: 1,
    endColumn: 26,
    suggestions: null
  }
]
```

```
1 !== 0

    at assertErrorCountIsCorrect (apps/oxlint/dist/index.js)
    at assertValidTestCasePasses (apps/oxlint/dist/index.js)
    at runValidTestCase (apps/oxlint/dist/index.js)
    at apps/oxlint/dist/index.js
```

This needs to be either implemented properly, or these tests should be disabled. However, it is plausible that these failures are related to the scope manager instead?

Docs for impliedStrict: https://eslint.org/docs/latest/use/configure/language-options#specifying-parser-options
oxc-project/oxc#16862.title: linter/plugins: Handle/disable rule tests using impliedStrict

### What happened?

This test is not testing what it should be: https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/e0d8001acdf6f67979fa8cda009926023dddf93b/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/jsdoc/empty_tags.rs#L360-L364

The settings blob has no `"settings"` key, and so does ignorePrivate does not get set in the settings. The test therefore falls back to the default value of false. If I try to fix this, the test fails, so I think the implementation may have a bug?

You can see the original implementation of this test here: https://github.com/gajus/eslint-plugin-jsdoc/blob/81cdb0a160db46848eb301955b1673b98d15f7df/test/rules/assertions/emptyTags.js#L189-L217

I think ignorePrivate should not be considered when checking this rule:
https://github.com/gajus/eslint-plugin-jsdoc/blob/81cdb0a160db46848eb301955b1673b98d15f7df/docs/rules/empty-tags.md?plain=1#L31-L33

oxc-project/oxc#16915.body:
This may be non-standard because the [Prettier ignore docs](https://prettier.io/docs/ignore) don't mention it, but Prettier supports putting the `// prettier-ignore` comment on the same line as the code you want ignored ([example](https://github.com/oxidecomputer/console/blob/ff400c344450e0ca23366cd66fc9c1d737bdf4ed/app/pages/settings/SSHKeysPage.tsx?plain=1#L48)). Oxfmt supports this on the line before (#14808), but not on the same line. I think it would be perfectly reasonable to close this as a wontfix — just wanted to flag it in case full compatibility is the goal. This is the source of like 95% of the diff vs. Prettier when I tried oxfmt just now on the [oxidecomputer/console](https://github.com/oxidecomputer/console) codebase.

https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/d77e22d4d94dfd151265c2c78ac5c19623cd88d8/crates/oxc_formatter/src/formatter/comments.rs#L368-L376

oxc-project/oxc#17189.body:
### What version of Oxlint are you using?

1.34.0

### What command did you run?

N/A

### What does your `.oxlintrc.json` config file look like?

N/A

### What happened?

For example, on the id-length rule there is a `properties` value with an enum type:

```md
### properties

type: `"always" | "never"`

When set to `"never"`, property names are not checked for length.
When set to `"always"` (default), property names are checked just like other identifiers.
```

It should be able to know from the code that `always` is the default value, and render it like it does for any other type of option, e.g. this option on the same rule renders a `default` value:

```md
### min

type: `integer`

default: `2`

The minimum number of graphemes required in an identifier.
```

I think this may be difficult because of the way the schemars crate handles enums and their default values, and whether it properly exposes what the default value actually is for an enum in the generated JSON Schema.

You can see the relevant struct for the `id-length` config here:

https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/b2b87c608f1842b27be3a5cf37bae56aa1f7387f/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/eslint/id_length.rs#L55-L71

and for the enum definition here:

https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/b2b87c608f1842b27be3a5cf37bae56aa1f7387f/crates/oxc_linter/src/rules/eslint/id_length.rs#L28-L34

oxc-project/oxc#17330.body:
This is what I found while investigating this issue.

https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/b4f8b8a0306b32f5a009730447ad133efe281ded/tasks/coverage/snapshots/formatter_babel.snap#L18-L19

Given these settings, parse error occured:

Input:
```ts
makeBox<number>[a]
```

TSC emits 2 errors for this, but it requires type info.

> TS2365: Operator '>' cannot be applied to types 'boolean' and 'any[]'.
> TS2693: 'number' only refers to a type, but is being used as a value here.

Oxc just parses this as series of `BinaryExpression`.

Options:
```
Expand::Always
```

Output:

```ts
makeBox <
  number >
  [
    a,
  ];

```

Parse error is caused by `,` of `a,`.

### NOTE: `Expand::Always`

Currently we are mapping `Expand` to Prettier's `objectWrap`.

But Prettier only accepts `collapse` and `preserve` as variant.

> https://prettier.io/docs/options#object-wrap

`always` is introduced by Biome and Prettier does not have its counterpart.

If we find `objectWrap: always` in `.oxfmtrc`:

- Use `Expand::Always` for `oxc_formatter` path
- But just fallback to `objectWrap: preserve` when passing it to Prettier

This may lead some confusion in some day, but otherwise, Prettier throws validation error.

> Error: Invalid objectWrap value. Expected "collapse" or "preserve", but received "always".

### NOTE: Biome also fails idempotency

Input:

```ts
makeBox<number>[a]
```

Options:
```
Expand::Always
```

Output:
```ts
makeBox <
  number >
  [
    a
  ];

```

Biome does not have extra `,`.

However, when use it as input with the same option, Output:

```ts
(makeBox<number>)[a];

```

And `oxc_formatter` also follow the same behavior with the same input.

---

Perhaps we could just remove `Expand::Always` itself.

Since this lives in common-options in Prettier (even though used only in JS/TS), we need to tweak this every time...
oxc-project/oxc#17330.title: formatter: `Expand::Always` unstability

oxc-project/oxc#17371.body:
Hi,

For the rule unicorn/prefer-event-target in the original plugin they made a modification to allow EventEmitter from certain packages and in particular from `@angular/core` .

https://github.com/sindresorhus/eslint-plugin-unicorn/issues/1853

oxc-project/oxc#17443.body:
## Motivation

There is sometimes a need to add more explanations and information about an error than will fit in just the diagnostic message. It can also be helpful to include information about the error message itself. For example, information about why an error message was emitted and/or how to turn it off.

### Prior art

`miette` diagnostics (which `oxc_diagnostics` is based on) is heavily inspired by the `rustc` compiler diagnostics. The `rustc` compiler has support for a `note` field for diagnostics similar to how we support a `help` field. It simply changes the name of the label, but provides a distinct place to put additional information.

Here are some examples of notes in `rustc`:

```
warning: unused variable: `y`
 --> src/main.rs:4:9
  |
4 |     let y = x;
  |         ^ help: if this is intentional, prefix it with an underscore: `_y`
  |
  = note: `#[warn(unused_variables)]` (part of `#[warn(unused)]`) on by default
```

```
error[E0382]: use of moved value: `x`
 --> src/main.rs:4:13
  |
2 |     let x = "hello world".to_string();
  |         - move occurs because `x` has type `String`, which does not implement the `Copy` trait
3 |     f(x);
  |       - value moved here
4 |     let y = x;
  |             ^ value used here after move
  |
note: consider changing this parameter type in function `f` to borrow instead if owning the value isn't necessary
```

```
error[E0283]: type annotations needed
 --> src/main.rs:2:9
  |
2 |     let x = "hello world".into();
  |         ^                 ---- type must be known at this point
  |
  = note: cannot satisfy `_: From<&str>`
  = note: required for `&str` to implement `Into<_>`
help: consider giving `x` an explicit type
  |
2 |     let x: /* Type */ = "hello world".into();
  |          ++++++++++++
```

## Proposal

We add a new `note` field to the `OxcDiagnosticInner` struct:

```diff
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Eq, PartialEq)]
pub struct OxcDiagnosticInner {
    pub message: Cow<'static, str>,
    pub labels: Option<Vec<LabeledSpan>>,
    pub help: Option<Cow<'static, str>>,
+   pub note: Option<Cow<'static, str>>,
    pub severity: Severity,
    pub code: OxcCode,
    pub url: Option<Cow<'static, str>>,
}
```

As well as additional helper methods to use the field:

- `OxcDiagnostic.with_note()`
- `OxcDiagnostic.note()`

## Use cases

I don't have any specific use cases in mind yet for this field, but we can use this issue to start collecting them. I think there are many lint rules that could benefit from this field as a way of providing more context.
oxc-project/oxc#17443.title: Diagnostics: Add support for notes



### What happened?

The `no-null` rule was autofixed, leading to interface changes in some functions. Some return types changed from `something | null` to `something | undefined` in the code, but the type annotation has not been updated (as it should).

for example (see [this commit](https://github.com/Ragnar-Oock/colon/commit/2906e34ebbcb44050a0a51a3ccd0ca95451ff9fb#diff-62e5193ee1177c99fb899273f6de46a81118e1ab13b086bf11e5f8306a6672e4L143)): 
```diff
const scoreContributors = computed<(Cell & { score: number, bonus: number })[] | undefined>(() => {
	const card = deck.active;
	const position = board.hoveredCell;
	if (card === null || position === null || !canPlace(cells.value, card, position)) {
-		return null;
+		return;
	}

	const contributors = getScoreContributors(card, position, cells.value);

-	return contributors.length === 0 ? null : contributors;
+	return contributors.length === 0 ? undefined : contributors;
})
```

Both return changed, but the type annotation on the first line didn't, leading to a TS error.

Running `oxlint --fix` should not have changed that, only `oxlint --fix-dangerous` should (if anything).
oxc-project/oxc#17466.title: linter: `no-null` should not be autofixed with just `--fix`

oxc-project/oxc#17467.body:
### Input

```tsx
/*
 * hi
 */
import cn from 'classnames'
import type { Hello } from 'pkg'
```

Notably it only happens when the type import is below and needs to be moved up. If the type import is before the other one, oxfmt does not move it into the comment.

### Config

```jsonc
{
  "experimentalSortImports": {}
}
```


### Oxfmt output

Oxfmt version: `0.21.0`

```tsx
/*
 * hi
import type { Hello } from "pkg";

 */
import cn from "classnames";
```


### Oxfmt playground link

https://playground.oxc.rs/#

### Prettier output

Prettier output is irrelevant because oxfmt is clearly wrong, plus Prettier doesn't sort imports natively

### Prettier playground link

_No response_

### Additional notes

Create the following `.oxfmtrc.json`

```
{
  "experimentalSortImports": {}
}
```

and the following one-liner will repro the issue:

`echo -e "/*\n * hi\n */\nimport cn from 'classnames'\nimport type { Hello } from 'pkg'" | npx oxfmt@0.21.0 --stdin-filepath f.ts`
oxc-project/oxc#17467.title: formatter: type import moved inside multiline header comment

oxc-project/oxc#17469.body:
### What version of Oxlint are you using?

1.36.0

### What command did you run?

`oxlint -c .oxlintrc.json`

### What does your `.oxlintrc.json` config file look like?

```jsonc
{
  "$schema": "./node_modules/oxlint/configuration_schema.json",
  "plugins": [
    "typescript"
  ],
  "categories": {
    "correctness": "off"
  },
  "rules": {
    "no-useless-constructor": "error"
  }
}
```


### What happened?

The `eslint(no-useless-constructor)` rule does not correctly detect how the arguments are being processed.

For example, when implementing a `ClassDecorator` where the constructor arguments are being modified, `oxlint --fix` will remove the constructor implementation entirely.

### Example

In the following example `@UseMetrics()` decorator is modifying the constructor arguments but is incorrectly being identified as a useless constructor:

```
  × eslint(no-useless-constructor): Redundant super call in constructor
    ╭─[src/use-metrics.decorator.ts:25:11]
 24 │         class extends target {
 25 │           constructor(...args: any[]) {
    ·           ─────┬─────
    ·                ╰── This constructor is unnecessary,
 26 │             super(...args.map((v, idx) => (idx === metricsIndex && isMetrics(v) ? v.child(name) : v)));
    ·             ─────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────
    ·                                                          ╰── because it only passes arguments through to the superclass
 27 │           }
    ╰────
  help: Subclasses automatically use the constructor of their superclass, making this redundant.
        Remove this constructor or add code to it.
```
oxc-project/oxc#17469.title: linter: `eslint(no-useless-constructor)` does not recognise argument processing

oxc-project/oxc#17499.body:
See this comment from Cam: https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/issues/15607#issuecomment-3584789880

> Unfortunately, at present, I've decided that we're not going to support the react compiler eslint rules.
> 
> The reason for this, is that the eslint plugin react compiler eslint rules, work by directly integrating with the react compiler.
The error cateogires that the react compiler emits then dynamically generates the eslint rules.
> 
> https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/408b38ef7304faf022d2a37110c57efce12c6bad/compiler/packages/babel-plugin-react-compiler/src/CompilerError.ts#L772
> 
> To be more specific, all of the rules run by invoking the react compiler, and getting a bunch of events back::
> 
> https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/3e1b34dc51dfc3cbada2cdd0ead5acee6998f444/packages/eslint-plugin-react-hooks/src/shared/ReactCompiler.ts#L112
>
> (makeRule is called for every rule, which calls getReactCompilerResult for every file being linted)
> 
> Within getReactCompilerResult, it re-parses the file, with babel, performs a babel transform, and during this transform, records the evens needed to report the lint errors.
>
> Given this, for us to have compat with the eslint plugin, we could effectivly need to mport the react compiler itself to rust, which feels like a huge undertaking, as well as maintence burden. From my understanding, the react compiler is stll actively being developed, which means we'd have the additional cost of keeping up, and keeping in sync with it.
> 
> Thanks for understanding.

Basically, we should update the react plugin tracker issue to ensure that we mark all react compiler rules from react-hooks as not-supported in the NOT_SUPPORTED_RULES list.

https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/blob/2e563e0858f0b7de761114c2766830e9d5ad3430/tasks/lint_rules/src/oxlint-rules.mjs

You can see the list of compiler rules here: https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/65eec428c40d542d4d5a9c1af5c3f406aecf3440/packages/eslint-plugin-react-hooks/README.md#flat-config-eslintconfigjsts-1
oxc-project/oxc#17499.title: linter: Add react compiler rules from react plugin to not-supported-rules list.

oxc-project/oxc#17501.title: linter: `import/extensions` erroneously throws on bare specifiers

oxc-project/oxc#17503.body:
With the rule name as the key and the value being a description of why it's not going to be ported to oxlint as a native rule.

Then we can use the explanation values for populating the auto-generated issues table, and also potentially use this info for the oxlint/migrate script's output? 

For example, when you run the migration script, you could get a message for rules matching the JSON file like `"react/foo" is unsupported because "the react/foo rule is deprecated upstream". It will not be implemented in oxlint at this time.`

Before and after:

```js
const NOT_SUPPORTED_RULE_NAMES = new Set([
  // ESLint rules that are deprecated in ESLint and replaced by rules in eslint-plugin-n:
  "eslint/no-process-env", // replaced by node/no-process-env, which we already support
  "eslint/no-new-require", // replaced by node/no-new-require, which we already support
  "eslint/no-buffer-constructor", // replaced by node/no-deprecated-api
  "eslint/no-path-concat", // replaced by node/no-path-concat
  "eslint/no-sync", // replaced by node/no-sync
  "eslint/no-process-exit", // replaced by node/no-process-exit
  "eslint/no-restricted-modules", // replaced by node/no-restricted-require
  "eslint/no-mixed-requires", // replaced by node/no-mixed-requires
  "eslint/global-require", // replaced by node/global-require
  "eslint/handle-callback-err", // replaced by node/handle-callback-err
  "eslint/callback-return", // replaced by node/callback-return
]);
```

```json
{
  "unsupported_rules": {
    "eslint/no-process-env": "deprecated, replaced by node/no-process-env, which we already support",
    "eslint/no-new-require": "deprecated, replaced by node/no-new-require, which we already support",
    "eslint/no-buffer-constructor": "deprecated, replaced by node/no-deprecated-api",
    "eslint/no-path-concat": "deprecated, replaced by node/no-path-concat",
    "eslint/no-sync": "deprecated, replaced by node/no-sync",
    "eslint/no-process-exit": "deprecated, replaced by node/no-process-exit",
    "eslint/no-restricted-modules": "deprecated, replaced by node/no-restricted-require",
    "eslint/no-mixed-requires": "deprecated, replaced by node/no-mixed-requires",
    "eslint/global-require": "deprecated, replaced by node/global-require",
    "eslint/handle-callback-err": "deprecated, replaced by node/handle-callback-err",
    "eslint/callback-return": "deprecated, replaced by node/callback-return"
  }
}
```

Related: #15927

oxc-project/oxc#17506.body:
See https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc/pull/17199#issuecomment-3678808825, #17479, and #17199.

This issue is for step 2 of this process.

Convert 1 or 2 rules to start returning an actual Result, and implement the error handling in the config builder (probably want to store all errors, then propagate them up via the CLI).

We want to surface errors to the user when they run the linter, if their config options are invalid (as in, they do not parse).

For step 3, which will be a separate issue/PR after this one, we'll want to start converting more rules to use this system, once we figure out a good solution via this issue. Those rules that get converted - for safety reasons - should _only_ be the rules that use `DefaultRuleConfig<>`, as those are guaranteed to already have correct config options defined in their schema.
oxc-project/oxc#17506.title: linter: Convert one or two rules to return a Result with actual error handling

oxc-project/oxc#17519.title: linter: JS Plugin rules are not included in the rule count printed at the end of a rule run

oxc-project/oxc#17522.body:
### What version of Oxlint are you using?

1.36.0

### What command did you run?

`npx oxlint  --report-unused-disable-directives --fix`

### What does your `.oxlintrc.json` config file look like?

```jsonc
{
  "$schema": "./node_modules/oxlint/configuration_schema.json",
  "jsPlugins": ["eslint-plugin-react-refresh"],
  "plugins": [],
  "rules": {
    "react-refresh/only-export-components": [
      "error",
      { "allowConstantExport": true }
    ]
  }
}
```

### What happened?

IDE flags `oxlint-disable` as unused and removes it automatically if configured to report unused disable directives and apply fixes on save.

I'm not actually sure there's anything that can be done about this, but I think there's two possible problems with it.
1. It's pretty annoying to deal with when trying to use JS plugins. Maybe this is just how it works until JS plugins are supported within the language server.
2. If I am in a codebase using both eslint and oxlint, oxlint will remove the eslint directives as it thinks they are unused because oxlint isn't configured for that rule but eslint is.
